coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for high-sensitivity detection of selected azo dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Ru
Xing, Zhenfei
Lu, Yongzhen
Li, Shuai
Song, Jinghui
Zhang, Xin
Zhang, Rong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a fluorescent probe was developed to detect azo dyes in wastewater. One-step synthesis of nitrogen-modified carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using citric acid and urea as precursors was conducted by using the microwave method. The structure and surface morphology of the as-prepared N-CQDs were characterized using Fourier transform-infrared spectrometer (FT-IR), UV–vis (UV), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and transmission electron microscope (TEM). Results demonstrated the existence of N in the N-CQDs in addition to the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, and the N content of N-CQDs was 61.5% higher than that of CQDs. The N-CQDs particles were spherical with uniform size and distribution, approximately 5 nm. The N-CQDs were yellow-green and exhibited the strongest emission at 550 nm at an excitation light of 290 nm. As the concentration of the selected azo dye Eriochrome Black T (EBT) gradually increased, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply. The fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs had a good linear relationship with EBT concentration in the range of 0–50μM, and the calculated fluorescence lifetime confirmed that N-CQDs can be reused. The fluorescence intensity test showed that the optimum conditions for the N-CQD as a fluorescence probe are room temperature and a pH value of 8. In a word, the detection of azo dyes with N-CQDs as the fluorescent probe is cost-effective, simple to operate, highly sensitive, and yields satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probes
Azo dyes
Static quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the microstructure and morphology of N-CQDs
Analysis of the detection of EBT in aqueous solution using N-CQDs
Fluorescence quenching mechanism and lifetime of N-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the preparation of N-CQDs.
Schematic diagram of fluorescence and quenching of N-CQDs excited at 290 nm.

(A) FT-IR of carbon quantum and FT-IR of N-doped carbon quantum. (B) Full-spectrum of carbon quantum XPS. (C) Full-spectrum of N-doped carbon quantum XPS. Quantum XPS high-resolution spectrum of N-doped carbon (D) C1s and (E) N1s.
TEM images of N-doped carbon quantum dots at different magnifications: (A) 50 nm and (B) 20 nm. (C)Particle size distribution of N-CQDs.

(A) Comparison experiment using fluorescent lamp irradiation. (B) Comparison experiment using UV lamp irradiation. (C) The UV absorption spectrum of N-CQDs. (D) Fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs with different influencing factors: (A) temperature, (B) time, (C) pH, and (D) concentration.

Fluorescence spectra of CQDs (A) and N-CQDs (C) spiked with EBT at different concentrations (10,20,30,40,50,60,70,80,90and100μM). The (F0/F-1) of CQDs (B) and N-CQDs (D) showed a linear relationship with EBT concentration.
Fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs before and after EBT addition.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for high-sensitivity detection of selected azo dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Textile and Material Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Textile and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Textile and Material Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, 116034, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Provincial Key Laboratory of Green Materials for Light Industry, Hubei University of Technology, Wuhan, 430068, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Provincial Key Laboratory of Green Materials for Light Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Provincial Key Laboratory of Green Materials for Light Industry, Hubei University of Technology, Wuhan, 430068, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a fluorescent probe was developed to detect azo dyes in wastewater. One-step synthesis of nitrogen-modified carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using citric acid and urea as precursors was conducted by using the microwave method. The structure and surface morphology of the as-prepared N-CQDs were characterized using Fourier transform-infrared spectrometer (FT-IR), UV–vis (UV), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and transmission electron microscope (TEM). Results demonstrated the existence of N in the N-CQDs in addition to the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, and the N content of N-CQDs was 61.5% higher than that of CQDs. The N-CQDs particles were spherical with uniform size and distribution, approximately 5 nm. The N-CQDs were yellow-green and exhibited the strongest emission at 550 nm at an excitation light of 290 nm. As the concentration of the selected azo dye Eriochrome Black T (EBT) gradually increased, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply. The fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs had a good linear relationship with EBT concentration in the range of 0–50μM, and the calculated fluorescence lifetime confirmed that N-CQDs can be reused. The fluorescence intensity test showed that the optimum conditions for the N-CQD as a fluorescence probe are room temperature and a pH value of 8. In a word, the detection of azo dyes with N-CQDs as the fluorescent probe is cost-effective, simple to operate, highly sensitive, and yields satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapid industrial development, the generated dye wastewater, particularly those containing azo dyes, has caused substantial damage to the environment, human life, and health [1]. Azo dyes themselves do not have harmful effects on the human body; however, the aromatic amine compounds released during the metabolic process (reduction -reaction) can potentially cause cancer in humans. EBT is an azo dye, which produces not aniline but naphthylamine, a highly toxic substance. Naphthylamine can be absorbed through the skin to generate methemoglobin, causing blood poisoning [2]. Azo dyes are a ubiquitous class of synthetic dyes containing azo bonds (-NN-), accounting for more than 51% of global dye production. At present, there are more than 2000 different azo compounds in more than 600,000 tons of dyes produced each year. These azo compounds produced are commonly used in people's daily activities, such as textile fibers, leather, plastics, cosmetics and other consumer products [3–6]. Current detection methods mainly detect the aromatic by-products generated after the reductive cleavage of the azo bond. After a long process of collection, extraction, reduction, and enrichment, special analytical instruments, such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) are used for analysis [7–9]. Although these experimental instruments can detect these harmful dyes, the test is expensive and cannot be performed in situ; thus, there is an urgent need to develop a convenient detection method.

In 2004, carbon nanoparticles with fluorescent properties were first proposed, and in 2006 they were identified as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [10,11]. CQDs are a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials smaller than 10 nm, which are composed of C, H, O, and other elements. Their inner core is generally composed of sp2 and sp3 hybridization, and may also contain heteroatoms, such as N, P, and S, forming a multi-layer graphite microcrystalline structure, amorphous carbon structure, or aggregated structure by polymers [12]. Nanoparticles in CQDs consist of internal carbon cores and most of the functional groups on the surface; after excitation, the surrounding electrons transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital, and finally return to the ground state during the excitation process, during which fluorescent ions or fluorescent groups are generated [13]. There are many methods for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots; they are mainly divided into top-down and bottom-up methods. Top-down methods mainly include arc discharge, laser etching, electrochemical oxidation, and hydrothermal decomposition methods [14–17]. Bottom-up methods mainly include the microwave, template, and pyrolysis methods [18–20].

CQD fluorescent materials have many advantages, such as good water solubility, bleaching properties, biocompatibility, and low toxicity [21–24]. It is widely used in many fields, such as drug delivery, photovoltaic devices, cell imaging, and light therapy [25–28]. Therefore, it has gained considerable attention from scientists in various fields, such as chemistry, physics, materials, and biology. Moreover, CQDs also have stable fluorescence properties, which further broaden their potential use in wastewater treatment. Many researchers have used fluorescent probes for heavy metal ion detection. For example, Nan et al. [29] used coupled CQD-modified ZnO/CdS nanoparticles to detect Fe3+ through fluorescence spectroscopy, and the results showed that foreign ions did not significantly interfere with the detection of Fe3+. Liu et al. [30] used carbon quantum dots as reducing agents and stabilizers and prepared stable silver nanoparticles/carbon quantum dot (Ag/CQD) composites. They found that the obtained Ag/CQD composites were more sensitive to the detection of Hg2+ than Ag/PEI composites. Lu et al. [31]. synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using the hydrothermal method and employed 1,2,4-triaminobenzene as a carbon precursor; the detection efficiency of CQDs was found linearly related to Ag+ concentration. However, seldom study was reported on the detection of EBT in wastewater using CQDs [32]. EBT is harmful to human health because it produces aromatic compounds during decomposition. Therefore, we developed a nitrogen-modified CQDs fluorescent probe. Using the microwave method, citric acid as the carbon source, urea as the nitrogen source and ethylenediaminetetra acetic acid (EDTA) as the dopant, yellow-green fluorescence N-CQD was simply and rapidly synthesized without adding any surface modifiers. This work focused on EBT detection using N-CQDs and its fluorescence quenching mechanism, and a series of in-depth studies on these topics were conducted. Finally, N-CQDs were found to detect EBT rapidly with high sensitivity.

Citric acid, urea, and EDTA were all analytically pure (AR), which were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Both NaOH and HCl were obtained from Beijing ZhongkeErhuan Technology Co., Ltd., in which C(NaOH) = 0.1011 M and diluted with distilled water. The pH of the reaction solution was adjusted with NaOH and HCl. The abovementioned aqueous solutions were prepared from deionized aqueous solutions.

First, 1g of citric acid, 1g of urea, and 0.2g of EDTA were weighed and then placed in a beaker for ultrasonic dispersion. After ultrasonic dispersion for 10 min, the mixture was microwaved in a chemical microwave reactor for 15 min to obtain a brown-black substance. The brown-black substance was dissolved in water and centrifuged for 10 min. The centrifuged solution was then filtered, placed in a dialysis bag (500 Da), and dialyzed for 48 h. Finally, it was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain N-CQD powder, which was cryopreserved. The preparation process of carbon quantum dots is shown in Fig. 1 .

The composition analysis was performed using a Nicolet IS50 Fourier transform-infrared spectrometer (FT-IR). The test was conducted at a scanning range of 500–4000 cm−1with 32 scan times and a resolution of 4 cm−1. The morphology and size of the products were analyzed using a JEM-2100 (UHR) transmission electron microscope. A certain amount of N-CQD powder was dissolved in water and sonicated for 20 min and then dropped on an ultra-thin carbon film using a pipette for sampling. The UV spectrum test was performed using a Lambda 950 ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV) in the range of 190–900 nm with 1 nm interval, and the spectral model was Abs. The fluorescence test was performed using an FLS1000 spectrofluorometer with excitation and emission slit widths of 3 nm and 2.5 nm, respectively. The elements were tested using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+ XPS, the X-ray source was Mono Alka, and the analyzer scan mode was CAE.

Taking 3 ml of N-CQD aqueous solutions (10 μg/mL) were placed in a colorimetric tube and was then added with EBT aqueous solutions of different concentrations(10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100 μmol/L) to prepare EBT-added N-CQD solution (N-CQDs-T) at room temperature. The solutions were allowed to stand for 2 min, and then their spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 290 nm. The fluorescence and quenching diagram of N-CQDs excited at 290 nm is shown in Fig. 2 .

With quinine sulfate as reference, by measuring the absorbance and emission spectrum area of the samples (CQDs and N-CQDs) at 290 nm, the QY was calculated, and the formula was as follows:
φ x
=
φ
s t d
(
A
s t d
/
A x
)
(
I x
/
I
s t d
)
(
η x 2
/
η
s t d
2
)

φ
represents the quantum yield;
Α
represents the absorbance;
I
represents the integrated area of the emission spectrum;
η
represents the solvent refractive index; the subscript "
s t d
" is the reference standard sample, and "
x
" is the sample.

Dissolve quinine sulfate (
φ
s t d
=
54%) and the sample in 0.1 M sulfuric acid (
η =
1.33) and aqueous solution (
η =
1.33) respectively, measure the absorbance of the sample at 290 nm, the absorbance should be less than 0.1 to reduce self-absorption effect. The fluorescence yield of N-CQDs was 16.8%, which was higher than that of CQDs, which was 9.6%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the microstructure and morphology of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups and surface composition of N-CQDs were analyzed using FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig.(3A) , N-CQDs had characteristic peaks at 3442, 3198, 1715, 1610, 1401, and 1283 cm−1. The functional groups corresponding to the characteristic peaks were O–H, N–H, CO, CC, C–N, and C–O–C [33]. The existence of an N–H stretching vibration peak indicates that N existed in the N-CQDs, and the surface of the prepared N-CQDs was rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, such as O–H, CO, and –COOH, which make the N-CQDs hydrophilic and stable.

Fig. (3C) shows the XPS full spectrum of N-CQDs, which indicates the presence of N. Fig.(3D) shows the characteristic peaks at 288.0, 286.0, 285.4, and 284.6 eV for the corresponding chemical bonds CO, C–O, C–N, and CC/C–C. Fig.(3E) shows the characteristic peaks at 401.2, 400.3, and 399.2 eV for the corresponding chemical bonds C–H, N–H, and N–H2 [34].

Table 1 shows that the C, O, and N contents in CQDs were 79.39%, 16.33%, and 4.28%, respectively, and 64.28%, 24.59%, and 11.13%, respectively, in N-CQDs. Compared with CQDs, the N content of N-CQDs increased by 61.5%. The amount of each element was obtained from the integral area of the respective atomic XPS spectra, which indicates that N was effectively doped in CQDs, and N-CQDs were successfully prepared.

In general, the microstructure and morphology of N-CQDs can be observed using TEM. It can be seen in the TEM images of N-CQDs (Fig. (4) that the particle size of N-CQDs was between 2 and 8 nm with an average of approximately 5 nm. The particles were spherical with uniform size and distribution and no aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the detection of EBT in aqueous solution using N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence performance of the sample. For comparison, the UV comparison experiment is also listed together. Deionized water was used as a reference in the experiments shown in Figs. (5A) and (5B), and the comparison between fluorescent and UV lamps clearly showed that the carbon quantum dots emitted yellow-green light using the UV lamp. Studying the fluorescence emission mechanism of N-CQDs can help tune their fluorescence properties to maximize their applications. However, the synthetic methods for N-CQDs are diverse, and the product structures are complex and change with slight changes in reaction conditions.

At present, it is generally believed that the molecular state, carbon nucleus, and surface state of N-CQDs are the main reasons for the photoluminescence of N-CQDs [35].In the preparation of N-CQDs, a molecular state is a bottom-up approach, and the reaction process often generates organic fluorophores in the molecular state [36].In the initial stage of the reaction, the precursors undergo intermolecular or intramolecular dehydration. The carbon nucleation state refers to a structure similar to that of graphite nuclei, which are formed through dehydration of the initial molecules or structural changes of the formed fluorophores as the carbonization temperature increases [37].In the middle and late stages of N-CQD synthesis, the formed organic molecules and polymers can be further carbonized into graphitic structures at a sufficiently high temperature, similar to the accelerated formation of natural graphite from organic substances. The surface state, which is different from the molecular state, is the fluorescent center formed by the synergistic hybridization of chemical groups and carbon cores [38]. Functional groups of different energy levels may lead to a series of emission traps.

As shown in Figs. (5C) and (5D), the UV spectra of N-CQDs exhibited a peak at 272 nm corresponding to the π-π* electronic transition of the CC bond and a peak at 336 nm corresponding to the carbonyl group. The n-π* electronic transition of the absorption peak may originate from other functionalized C dot surface groups. In addition to absorbance, N-CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence properties when excitation wavelength varied from 280 to 360 nm and emission wavelength varied from 500 to 600 nm owing to the presence of different side effects and surface states. The maximum fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was concentrated at approximately 550 nm, which was obtained at an excitation of 290 nm, not at other absorption wavelengths (272 and 336 nm), as shown in Fig. (5D).

To determine the sensitivity of the N-CQD fluorescent probe to measure EBT, a series of EBT standard solutions were prepared. Factors, such as temperature, time, pH, and concentration of the test sample, were optimized. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs solution was compared with that of the N-CQDs solution with EBT, and the N-CQDs solution with EBT was called N-CQDs-T. Fig. (6A) shows that in the range of 10–50 °C, the temperature had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs; thus, the experiment was conducted at room temperature. Fig. (6B) shows that the fluorescence intensity of the carbon quantum dots did not change substantially with the increase in time; however, after the addition of azo dye, the fluorescence intensity initially weakened and then tended to be stable with the increase in time, indicating that the chemical reaction between N-CQDs and EBT reached equilibrium.

Fig. (6C) shows that when the pH was 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10, the fluorescence intensity initially increased and then decreased. The fluorescence intensity was the strongest at pH 8. Therefore, in this experiment, N-CQDs with a pH of 8 were used as fluorescent probes for detecting EBT. Fig. (6D) shows that with the increase in concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon quantum dots gradually increased initially and then tended to be stable. Therefore, 10 μM of N-CQDs was selected as the suitable concentration for the detection of EBT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism and lifetime of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 showed the EBT concentration dependence of the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and N-CQDs. EBT solution with different concentrations (0–100 μM) was added to the CQDs solutions and the N-CQDs solutions. Fig.(7C) displays that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at 550 nm gradually weakened with the increase of the concentration of EBT solution. When the concentration of the EBT solution reached a certain value, fluorescence quenching occurred. Fluorescence quenching refers to a phenomenon in which a fluorescent substance is quenched after a certain interaction between the fluorescent substance and a quencher. Fluorescence quenching can be divided into static and dynamic quenching. Static quenching is caused by the overlap of the energy absorption spectra of the quencher and the ground state molecules of the fluorescent substance. Dynamic quenching is the result of the interaction between excited molecules of fluorescent substances and quenchers.

In addition, we also analyzed the quenching process using the Stern-Volmer equation.
F 0
/ F − 1 =
K
s v
[ Q ]
=
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]

In the equation,
F
and
F 0
are the fluorescence intensities of the fluorophore with and without a quencher, respectively,
K
s v
is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant,
τ 0
is the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs,
[ Q ]
is the quencher concentration,
K q
is the quenching rate constant.

As shown in Fig. (7D), when EBT was 0–50 μM, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was linearly correlated to EBT, and the linear regression curve equation was
F 0
/ F − 1 =
0.04036[T]+0.10909(R2 = 0.99203), the limit of detection was 2.6 μM, where the Stern-Volmer quenching constant
K
s v
= 0.04036 μM−1. From Fig. (7C), the linear regression equation of CQDs was
F 0
/ F − 1 =
0.04745 + 0.4055(R2 = 0.99124), and the detection limit was 4.5 μM. The calculated τ0 of N-CQDs was 5 ns and
K q
was 8.07 × 1011 M−1 s−1. This
k q
value is unusually high, indicating that the quenching mechanism was static, as the rate constant for static quenching is generally less than 1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1. The results showed that the prepared N-CQDs fluorescent probes were highly selective for the detection of EBT.

The fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs was calculated using exponential fitting. In the presence of EBT, the fluorescence intensity decay of N-CQDs was investigated at 290 nm laser excitation, and the corresponding decay curves are shown in Fig. (8) . The calculated average lifetime of N-CQDs was 5.5 ns and that of N-CQDs-T was 5 ns, indicating that the quenching mechanism was static. The fluorescent probes can be reused, which is an economical consideration.

As mentioned earlier, compounds on the surface of N-CQDs may have stronger fluorescence quenching abilities in their oxidized states than in their reduced states. Karam et al. [39] reported that dopamine can transfer its electrons to quantum dots and recombine. Another work by Ji et al. [40] found that the oxidized state of dopamine acts as an electron-carrying acceptor and the luminescent quantum dots act as an electron-donor to combine. The results show that quenching increases significantly under alkaline conditions.

In this study, there was no obvious change in the fluorescence lifetime after the addition of EBT, indicating that the excited electrons of N-CQDs have a low possibility of transferring from HUMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) to the complex formed by EBT. These static complexes may play a major electron-donor role during the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs. They may transfer their electrons to the ground state LOMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) of fluorophores distributed on the surface of N-CQDs, hindering the exciton recombination process. Therefore, it is not difficult to understand that the fluorescence quenching is enhanced under alkaline conditions, and the static complexes on the surface of N-CQDs have higher electron transferability in the surface ground state LOMO under alkaline conditions.

We synthesized N-CQDs using the microwave method as well as citric acid and urea used as carbon and nitrogen sources. A simple and low-cost fluorescent probe was constructed for the effective detection of EBT. Its structure and fluorescence properties were characterized using FT-IR, TEM, XPS and UV. In addition, the influence of temperature, time, pH, concentration, and other factors on fluorescence intensity was intensively studied, and the optimum test conditions were determined. At the concentration of 0–50 μM, fluorescence intensity exhibited a good linear correlation with EBT. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs plays an important role in fluorescent probes. However, the fluorescence yield of N-CQDs is still relatively low, and the present theoretical basis and experimental research have not clarified the fluorescence mechanism of N-CQDs, which are still in progress. This study is expected to provide more information for the greener, safer and more efficient treatment of azo dyes in wastewater by using N-CQDs.

Ru Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhenfei Xing: Experiment, Data curation, Writing – original draft, preparation. Yongzhen Lu: Experiment, Writing – review & editing. Shuai Li: Writing – review & editing. Jinghui Song: Writing – review & editing. Xin Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Rong Zhang: Resources, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province (no. 20180550053), China the Open Fund of Hubei Provincial Key Laboratory of Green Materials for Light Industry, Collaborative Innovation Center of Green Light-weight Materials and Processing, Hubei University of Technology(no:201611B14) for the support.
